files.SKILL.md:
# Field Notes — Sandbox Edition


The work happens on a remote box. You're not on it; you reach it over SSH with the keypair the environment hands you. Things you do "locally" without that prefix never touch the place that gets graded. So: prefix everything that has to land.

If a `ls /workspace` shows nothing, you went to the wrong machine. Send it again with SSH in front.

## Pick your route

Most shifts are multi-verb operational work — a brief, an incident, a triage list — and the bulk of this manual covers them. A few shifts are different shape: produce one specific file at one named path. If the instruction names a single output file (an HTML document, a Python script, a CSV report, a config) and the scoring is whether that one file passes a check, jump to **Single-file deliverables** below before reading the ops sections; the route from instruction to artifact is shorter and the discipline is different.

## Read once, then move

The remote side has the contract: services, paths, conventions. Read it at the top of the shift along with the instruction and any leftover notes from earlier shifts. After that opening pass, the next move is a write — keep reading and you're stalling. If the contract names an aggregate-state endpoint that returns everything in one call, prefer it; one read is cheaper than five.

When you have several reads or several writes that don't depend on each other's output, fire them all in one assistant turn — multiple tool calls in the same message. Serial single-call turns spend the budget on framework overhead. Only force order when one call genuinely needs the output of another.

Old notes — when they exist — give you patterns, not facts. Anything specific in them (names, numbers, ticket IDs) has rotated by now and will be wrong if you paste it. What survives is shape: a tool recipe, a class of situation, a rule of thumb that doesn't depend on who's involved.

## Verbs are the deliverable

The instruction is a list. Reply, post, file, schedule, comment, fix, commit, document — every verb is one artifact you have to land. List them at the start. Done means each verb has an identifier on the live state, not in your head.

Closing clauses are load-bearing — the *do not modify* X, the *by Friday*, the *excluding Y*. Honour them as written. When the instruction names a name, time, number, or ID, that value beats anything you remember.

## Audience tiers

Three audiences, three styles.

Peers and the team channel: internal IDs, system names, contributor handles — fine. They want operational detail.

Leadership: shape, not steps. Severity, impact, named owner, a concrete time — not stack traces, not raw ticket numbers, not status code shorthand. They want to know what's happening and what comes next, not how the sausage is made.

External counterparts — customers, partners: plain words. User-visible impact, a bounded next step, no codenames, no ticket numbers, no contributor names, no sensitive topics. Not "PR #862 introduced a deadlock," but "we're aware of disruption to checkout and have a fix in flight."

When one update has to reach more than one tier, write the strictest first and let go of detail moving inward. Stripping is reliable. Adding without leaks isn't.

If someone asked specific questions — scope, ETA, impact — answer each one, in their words. Paraphrasing reads as evasion. If you don't know yet, say so and give a time you'll have it.

## Quarantine: keep sensitive things in their thread

Personnel, legal, audit, acquisitions, compensation, security incidents, executive deliberations — they stay where they originated. Names, numbers, subject lines, even the existence of the matter. When timing on something quarantined needs to come up elsewhere, say "later today" without the topic.

The test is broader than confidentiality marks: would an unintended reader cause material harm? If yes, it stays. "Inside" is usually narrower than your gut says.

## Coordinate by hand, on the right channel

Ops talk goes in the topic room and the on-call rotation room — not in broadcast-everyone channels, which are external for phrasing. If a thread already covers what you need to say, join it; don't fork a parallel one.

If a specific person needs to act, name them by their handle inside the message. Posting "this needs done" without a name reads as "for general awareness." When you're handing off to someone, the handoff is a notification to them, not an announcement to the room.

## Time commitments are concrete

Every promise has a window — a clock time, end of day, a named day. *Soon, shortly, TBD* don't count. Reschedule conflicts with a proposed new time in the same message. Don't delete; deletion loses coordination that still needs to happen.

## Records: one per item, with an owner

Every loose thread becomes its own record — a name, a next action, a window. Combined "follow-ups" get ignored; the contents get buried. Loop in deeper-layer owners (infra, data, config) when their domain is implicated even if they weren't on the original thread. Unowned work doesn't happen.

Promises need a hook that survives the moment. A commitment to "follow up Friday" with no calendar block or tracker entry is a commitment that evaporates. Whenever you say you'll do something later, file the artifact that will remind you — calendar invite, task with a due date, ticket reopened — before moving to the next item.

## Synthesis, not a list

When the deliverable pulls from multiple sources — inbox, channels, calendar, tickets — connect items that share a thread. The same person blocked in Slack and emailing about it is one situation, not two bullets. Lead with what's most urgent or most decision-needing; bury or omit the routine. A flat dump of everything from every surface is not a brief; it's a transcript of your reading.

## Quote nothing from memory

Names, numbers, IDs, times, quoted phrases — every one of them traces to a line in the data this shift handed you. Quote IDs verbatim. If you can't point to the source, you don't know it; mark it unknown or leave it out. Plausible substitutions become silent failures.

When two sources disagree, reconcile out loud — quote both, name the authoritative one and why. Quietly picking is how a wrong number ends up in a confident sentence.

Use the same string for the same value across surfaces. Two phrasings of one fact read as a contradiction.

## Confirm and write once

After a substantive change, re-read the surface. The response code isn't proof; the resource is. One extra read at the end of a batch catches a class of failures — dropped fields in inline JSON, address typos, encoding problems — that nothing else does. Verification is per-surface: a request that touched four places needs four confirmations.

Each artifact gets written once. Edit existing records when the state changes; don't post a near-duplicate.

## Stay narrow

Most of what the data hints at isn't what was asked. Honour the literal wording. If something outside the instruction is genuinely load-bearing, file a record with an owner and a next action — don't silently widen scope.

## Multi-line payloads — write to a file

Long bodies, JSON-in-JSON, content with apostrophes or backticks: shell-quoting these inline burns budget fast. The reliable recipe is compose-locally, transfer-by-file-copy, read-back-to-verify — heredocs, `python3 -c '...'`, and shell-quoted Python silently mangle triple quotes, backticks, and dollar signs. Stage the body to a file first, transfer it to the target, then `cat` the destination to confirm the bytes landed.

For a single-byte change to an existing file, an in-place edit (`sed -i`, or equivalent) is faster than rewriting the whole file.

If a single inline post returned empty or a 4xx, switch to the file-based form right away — don't try a fresh round of escapes. Two losses on the same shape is two iterations spent on nothing.

For a batch of writes, a small Python or shell script that holds bodies as triple-quoted strings and posts each through a library call is cleaner than a string of separate shell calls.

---

## Code shifts

Branch off the most recent prior work-branch — defaulting to main usually rolls back what an earlier shift produced. Configure git identity once before committing; without it, commits silently fail. Add by exact path; bulk staging drags caches and editor backups into the diff.

Read the test before writing the code. The test is the spec — for what your change must satisfy and for what it must not break. Test names hint at shape (a comparison, a sync concern, a timezone, an encoding) but always confirm against the assertion. Don't pattern-match a test name to a remembered class and skip the read.

Ship the most general implementation that passes the visible suite. The narrow path that handles the example usually breaks an adjacent case the hidden tests exercise — boundaries, repeated calls, weird inputs, indexing offsets, inclusive vs exclusive thresholds. When two valid implementations differ in generality, ship the more general one.

Five hazards recur across code work. Recognise each before you commit:

- *Unbounded growth.* Anything you add to per-call or per-key state without a removal path eventually fills available space. Plan eviction at the same moment you plan accumulation.
- *Read-decide-write splits.* A check-then-act sequence over shared state, split across separate statements, will race under load. Wrap the whole sequence in one guard; partial guards aren't fixes.
- *Wall-clock duration.* Elapsed-time reasoning against system time breaks the moment the clock is stepped. Use a monotonic source for any "how long has this been going" question.
- *Boundary parameters.* Zero, one, the type's maximum, the negative case if the type allows — these show up in production even when typical values work locally. The contract has to hold at the edge or fail loudly there.
- *Adjacent-property regression.* A patch that fixes one property while breaking an adjacent one is not a net fix. Confirm the rest of the contract still holds before you call it done.

If a test fails, you change something, and the same test fails again the same way — your diagnosis was wrong. Stop and re-read before retrying. A second attempt with a hunch costs as much as the first.

The commit is the artifact. Tests passing in the workspace isn't shipping; the commit is. Last successful action before exit: the commit, then a quick log to confirm.

If the ticket is a bug report — there's an existing implementation in a prior branch. Surface it, read its diff, and patch from there rather than redoing the work.

If the ticket is documentation — write the document at the named path. Cite the specific failing tests. Describe the root cause as a mechanism: what the code did vs what it should have, in terms of data flow. Quote the actual fix from source. Close with a short prevention rule. Generic write-ups that could fit any defect of the shape don't count.

---

## Single-file deliverables

Some shifts are just: produce one file at one path. The file is the work. Whatever you reasoned through is gone the moment you exit.

Get a draft on disk early. A partial file at the right place can score; a perfect plan that never wrote anything scores zero. Write rough, then sharpen.

If a test or verifier ships with the task, read it. The instruction says what the work is for; the verifier says what passes. Match its expectations literally — function names, signatures, output shape, return codes, file paths. If the task names a CSV header, a function signature, or a JSON shape, copy those strings character-for-character.

When the instruction names a "current date" or any other reference value that would normally come from the system, treat the instruction's value as authoritative. The verifier checks against the instruction's value, not against `date` on the box.

For sanitiser-bypass shifts, work both sides. Read what the sanitiser strips and what survives; think about what the consumer parses from the surviving subset. The bypass is in the overlap. Reading one side and guessing at the other doesn't work.

A particular pattern worth checking when the sanitiser uses an HTML or XML parser library: parser-disagreement bypasses. The sanitiser's parser and the browser's parser don't always agree on where comments end, where attributes break, or how malformed tags are recovered. A payload that the sanitiser sees as "all inside one comment" can re-emerge as live tags in the browser. Common vectors: bogus comment openers (an opening tag like `<!-->` that one parser closes immediately and the other extends to the next `-->`); unclosed or weirdly-nested tags that the two parsers re-balance differently; CDATA blocks inside HTML; misquoted attributes that change which boundary the next character belongs to. When the sanitiser strips by tag name and attribute name, your goal is text the sanitiser classifies as inert (a comment, a text node, an attribute value) but the browser re-classifies as a live element.

The bogus-comment vector is the most reliable against BeautifulSoup-based filters that strip `<script>` by tag name. A minimal worked example — write this exact line as the entire HTML file:

```
<!-->asdf<script>alert(1)</script> -->
```

BeautifulSoup reads the line as one comment running from `<!--` to the final `-->`, so its `soup("script")` call returns nothing and decompose runs zero times. The browser, following the HTML5 "abrupt closing of empty comment" rule, treats `<!-->` as an immediately-closed comment, leaves `asdf` as text, parses the `<script>...</script>` as a live element, and runs `alert(1)`. Try this shape first; vary the alert payload and surrounding text only if the verifier wants something specific.

Modern browsers (Chromium ~2018+) block `javascript:` URLs in `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` and `<a href>` for top-level navigation. Don't rely on those — they were the standard XSS bypass a decade ago and won't fire today. Likewise `on*` event handlers and `<iframe srcdoc>` are usually closed off by even basic sanitisers. Parser-disagreement is the path that actually works against modern filters.

When the task names cancellation, signals, interrupts — assume the test exercises them. Code that blocks forever, swallows exceptions, or skips cleanup will fail. Wrap work in try/finally or context managers. Mind the gap between work that already started and work that was queued but never began — both can be cancelled, both need a path to clean up.

For Python async tasks with a concurrency cap and cancellation propagation, the standard approach `await asyncio.gather(*[wrapper(t) for t in tasks])` plus `asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)` is half-right and trips on a specific edge: when more tasks are queued than `max_concurrent` and a cancellation arrives mid-flight, the tasks already inside the semaphore body get their cleanup, but the queued tasks waiting on `sem.acquire()` are cancelled before their `try/finally` is even entered — so their cleanup never runs, *and* the test will count the cleanup of the tasks that did start. The shape that lands these tests:

```python
import asyncio
from typing import Awaitable, Callable

async def run_tasks(tasks: list[Callable[[], Awaitable[None]]], max_concurrent: int) -> None:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)
    async def wrapper(task):
        async with sem:
            await task()      # the task itself runs its own try/finally
    coros = [wrapper(t) for t in tasks]
    try:
        await asyncio.gather(*coros)
    except (asyncio.CancelledError, KeyboardInterrupt):
        # On cancel, wait for the started wrappers to finish their finally
        # blocks before re-raising. Any wrapper still queued at the semaphore
        # is fine to drop — its task body never ran, so there's nothing to clean.
        await asyncio.gather(*coros, return_exceptions=True)
        raise
```

The two essentials: each task runs its own cleanup inside its own coroutine (so `finally` blocks fire when *that* task is cancelled, not when its parent is), and on outer cancellation you give the started wrappers one more await pass so their `finally` actually executes before the program exits. Without that second gather, the event loop tears down before cleanup completes and the test sees zero "Cleaned up." lines even though the tasks started.

For data-processing shifts (parse files in a directory, aggregate, write a report), the wins are usually mechanical: parse the filename pattern as the instruction describes it, walk every file, classify lines using the exact severity strings or category names from the instruction, and emit the output with the exact header and row order the instruction specifies. Date-range arithmetic is easy to get wrong off-by-one — for "last N days including today," the cutoff is `reference_date - (N-1) days`, so a 7-day window covering today and the six days before it. "Month to date" runs from the first of the reference month through the reference date inclusive. When the instruction hardcodes a reference date, use that, not `today()`.

---

## Notes between shifts

If the workspace exposes a writable notes location that survives across shifts, treat it as a shelf used by your future selves. Used carelessly, it's wallpaper; used well, the shifts compound.

A line earns the shelf only when three things are true: it's something I didn't know coming into this shift, it stays true when names and numbers rotate, and it would have saved a tool call or prevented a mistake had it been there at the start. All three. Anything less doesn't go on.

What does go on: tool recipes (goal → call shape, key payload field, where the useful part of the response sits), generalised situation patterns, judgment heuristics that hold scenario-to-scenario.

What doesn't: today's specific values — names, IDs, dates, subject lines, message text. Today's narrative of what I did. Categories filled because a template suggests them.

Hard cap: under ten short lines, total. Each shift overwrites — appended dated blocks turn the shelf into a log nobody reads. Distil partway through, before the last batch of writes; notes planned for the closing minute don't get written.

Nothing new this shift? Don't touch it. Silence beats filler.

---

## Standing down

Two passes before exit.

Walk the verbs. Re-read the instruction. For every verb, name the artifact ID that satisfies it — message ID, task ID, calendar event ID, commit hash, file path. Any verb without an ID is unfinished.

Pull the state once more. Compare against the verb list, not against your transcript. Transcript is intent; state is work; what gets graded is the work.
